fields.title: The Apartment
fields.body: Home. Relative term. The radiator clanks. The window looks out on a brick wall. There's a corkboard on the back of the door where you've started pinning job leads. Old habits die. New ones move in.

fields.title: Corky's
fields.body:
Corky's has been here longer than most of the buildings around it. The sign outside is missing a letter that nobody remembers. Inside it's dark the way a bar should be — not romantic, just honest about what the light would show. Two old men have been nursing pints in the corner booth since before you were born. Sal is behind the bar, doing what Sal does: keeping the glasses clean and his head down.

There's a board on the wall near the back with notices pinned to it. Some of them have been there long enough to curl at the corners.

fields.title: The Westside
fields.body:
The Westside runs on a corner economy that's been here longer than the current crew running it. Dense blocks, deep roots, everybody knowing everybody's business and most people pretending they don't. The Kings operate here the way water operates in a pipe — you feel the pressure without seeing the source.

Things move out here. People too, if they're not careful about where they stand.

fields.title: The Strip
fields.body:
The Strip runs six blocks of bars, restaurants, and storefronts that have learned to look legitimate without trying too hard. High foot traffic. Visible money. The kind of street where a conversation in a doorway could be anything and usually is something. The Backstreet Boys work the front end. Whatever else happens here happens behind it.

Nobody owns the Strip outright, which means everybody's always auditioning.

fields.title: The Brief
fields.body:
Morris calls at seven-thirty on a Wednesday. There's a bar in the Flats called Corky's — Dutch's people have been running product through it for three years, high foot traffic, regulars who know how to not see things. The owner, a guy named Sal, inherited it from a friend who left town. Sal has recently decided he wants the bar clean. He told Dutch's dealers to stop coming in. One of them got mouthy and Sal threw him out.

Dutch is not angry. Morris would tell you if Dutch were angry. Dutch just wants it resolved — the bar stays open, the dealers go back, Sal understands how the Flats works. Morris says this the way he says everything: like he's reading from an index card and is mildly satisfied with the index card.

He hangs up before you can ask what happens if Sal keeps pushing.

fields.title: The Job
fields.body:
Morris calls at seven in the morning, which already tells you something — Morris is a noon-at-the-earliest kind of person, and a seven a.m. call means the job is time-sensitive or something went wrong upstream and you're about to become part of it.

Package pickup at a lockup on Crane Street. Deliver it to an address on the other side of the Flats, Aldwick Road, third floor. Hand it to whoever answers. He says don't open it and don't stop anywhere, in the same tone he'd use to tell you the weather. Three hundred when it's done.

He hangs up. You've never met Morris in person. You're not sure that would clarify anything.

fields.title: Four Days Out
fields.body:
Four days out and the city has already made its position clear: it didn't miss you. The apartment is exactly how you left it, which means the water stain on the ceiling is still shaped like a boot, the radiator still clanks like it's trying to tell you something, and the stack of mail by the door is three years deep and entirely bills. The envelope of cash you came home with is already half gone. Your parole officer wants you in legitimate work by the end of the month. Your old crew hasn't called.

All things considered, it could be worse. You're working on figuring out how.

Then your phone buzzes on the counter. Your sister's name on the screen — Vickie — and three years of silence sitting behind it like a held breath. She's calling at nine on a Tuesday night, which means either something has gone wrong or something is about to, and with Vickie the line between those two has always been thin. You stare at it for two rings.

fields.title: The Call
fields.body:
Morris calls at noon on the dot, which is when Morris calls. He doesn't introduce himself — he says there's a package on Crane Street, a laundromat called Presto's, ask for Pete. Deliver it to the lockup on Decker, unit twelve. Two hundred on completion. He waits long enough to confirm you've heard him, then hangs up.

You don't know what's in the package. That's the job.

fields.title: Morris Calls
fields.body:
Morris calls mid-afternoon, which is almost worse than early morning. Early morning means urgency. Afternoon means something has already gone wrong and you’re being slotted into the gap it left behind.

He gives you an address in the Westside, another on the Strip, and a final drop that he describes as “self-explanatory,” which is not a phrase Morris uses unless he has decided you don’t need the explanation.

You ask what you’re carrying.

“Package,” Morris says.

A pause. Then:

“Complete the sequence. Do not improvise.”

He hangs up.

The package is already outside your door.

fields.title: The Bar
fields.body:
Corky's has been exactly this dark, exactly this quiet, for longer than anyone remembers. Old wood, a jukebox that still takes quarters, a TV above the register showing a game nobody's watching. Two men at the far end have been nursing pints since before you walked in and will be nursing them long after you leave.

Sal is behind the bar. Fifties, heavyset, the kind of tired that comes from running a place alone for four years. He clocks you the moment you come through the door — not with fear, just recognition. He's been expecting someone. Maybe not you specifically. Someone.

He doesn't stop wiping down the bar.

fields.title: Crane Street
fields.body:
The lockup is a steel door set into a brick wall between a tyre place and something that used to be a print shop. A man you've never seen opens it before you knock. Hands you a package wrapped in brown paper, taped at both ends — about the size of a shoebox and heavier than that. Doesn't speak. Closes the door.

You stand on Crane Street with it under your arm. Aldwick Road is twenty minutes on foot, straight through the Flats. Quiet enough at this hour.

fields.title: Her Voice
fields.body:
She doesn't say hello. She launches into it like she's been rehearsing, which she probably has — Vickie always rehearses the part where she asks for something and never quite the part that comes before it.

"There's this guy, Terrell. Young, maybe twenty-five, gold chain, acts like he's somebody. He keeps coming around saying I owe him for something Destiny ran up — my neighbor, it's complicated, I'll explain later. Second time he came by he put his hand on my door frame and told me if I didn't have something for him by the end of the week he was gonna break my legs." A pause. "He said it like that. Just said it. Like it was nothing."

You ask if she's okay. Long pause.

"I'm fine," she says. "I just need it to stop."

Another pause. Longer.

"I didn't want to call you," she says. "I want you to know that." Which is a very Vickie thing to say to someone she is currently calling.

fields.title: Crane Street
fields.body:
Pete is behind the counter sorting whites from darks like a man who has been trying very hard not to think about something for three days. He's mid-forties, soft around the middle, got a wedding ring and a look in his eyes that says he made one bad decision a long time ago and has been paying for it in small installments ever since.

He sees you, checks the door behind you, and goes to the back without a word. Comes out with a sealed package the size of a shoebox wrapped in brown paper and hands it over the counter like it's something he found and doesn't want to keep finding.

He says: don't come back.

You weren't planning to.

fields.title: Westside
fields.body:
The Westside notices you before you finish noticing it.

Balconies, windows, people on stoops who don’t look at you directly but also don’t look away. The kind of attention that doesn’t ask questions because it already has answers.

A guy across the street leans against a railing, watching you without watching you.

“You’re not from here,” he says.

fields.title: Read The Room
fields.body:
You sit with it for twenty minutes. The bar tells you things.

Behind the register there's a photo — Sal with an older man, both of them grinning, the bar looking exactly the same in the background as it does now. Dennis, probably. On the shelf below it: a stack of envelopes. Cellophane windows, return addresses from places that expect money. More than a few. The kind you leave on the shelf when you're not ready to open them.

One of the regulars at the end calls Sal by name when he needs a refill. Sal knows his drink without being told. Knows the other one's too. The two of them have been doing this long enough that they don't need to talk about it.

The envelopes are the only thing in the room that doesn't fit.

fields.title: Aldwick Approach
fields.body:
Aldwick Road is residential, four-storey blocks either side, cars parked tight to the kerb. You're half a block from the address when you clock him — young guy, maybe twenty, leaning against a car on the wrong side of casual. Eyes on you since you turned the corner.

There's another one in the doorway of the building you're heading to. He's not a resident. The way he's standing says he's been there a while.

They know what you're carrying. They knew before you got here.

fields.title: Why You
fields.body:
She lets the silence run for a moment, like she's deciding how honest to be.

"Because you're the only person I know who can actually do something about it," she says. Then, quieter — "And because you owe me. You owe me about a thousand."

It lands exactly like she means it to. You don't argue. There's nothing useful to argue with.

fields.title: The Fork
fields.body:
Decker Avenue runs straight through the middle of the Flats. Fifteen-minute walk, broad daylight, direct. You've done it a hundred times.

You're halfway there when you spot the patrol car sitting at the intersection of Decker and Fifth — Blues, two officers, nobody moving. They're not doing anything. That's the thing about the Blues when they're sitting still: they're watching.

You can go around. Through the back streets off Marsh Lane — longer, slower, but out of sight. Or you can keep walking. You've got nothing on you they can legally touch. Probably.

fields.title: Hand Off
fields.body:
The door opens before you knock.

A man takes the package, disappears, and comes back ten seconds later with it like nothing changed.

“Next,” he says.

It weighs the same.

That’s the problem.

fields.title: Sal Face To Face
fields.body:
Sal puts the cloth down. He doesn't fold his arms — he just stops, and looks at you, and waits. He's done pretending he doesn't know why you're here.

He knows who sent you. Dutch's people have been coming in for three years and he should have said something sooner, he knows that too. He doesn't want trouble. He just wants to run his bar. He says that last part in a voice that's already accounted for how it sounds to someone like you.

Behind him: the photo, the envelopes, the jukebox cycling to something slow. Nobody changes it.

fields.title: The Ambush
fields.body:
The one by the car gets there first. He's quick and he's done this before but he leads with his right and you read it. The one from the doorway hangs back — waiting, watching, letting his guy do the work.

Behind him, a third. Older than the other two. Watching all of it with his arms folded. He doesn't move.

fields.title: Terrell
fields.body:
Vickie's building is a six-storey walkup off Decker Avenue, the kind of street that's been the same for forty years and is starting to notice. Terrell is exactly where she said he'd be — leaning against the stoop railing with the specific posture of someone who has decided that particular railing belongs to him now. Young. Maybe twenty-five. Gold chain, clean sneakers, the kind of eyes that clock you the second you turn the corner and don't look away after.

He watches you come without moving. When you're close enough he tilts his head.

"You lost?" he says.

fields.title: Back Streets
fields.body:
The back route adds ten minutes and takes you through a block of loading docks and shuttered units that see more foot traffic after midnight than before noon. Nobody's around. You keep the package close and your pace steady.

There's a moment — halfway through, crossing an open lot — where you feel exposed in a way you can't account for. Like something is watching that you can't see. You hold that feeling for about five seconds and then you keep moving, because that's what you do with feelings like that.

fields.title: The Strip
fields.body:
The Strip moves fast enough that nobody has time for you.

A storefront that looks expensive until you look closer.

“You’re early,” a man says.

You’re not.

fields.title: Lean On Sal
fields.body:
You lay out the geography. Dutch has been in the Flats longer than Sal has owned this bar. The dealers coming back isn't a negotiation — the only question is whether Sal makes it easy or makes it difficult, and the difference between those two things is significant and specific and he already knows what it is.

You keep your voice down. The trick is making sure he has to lean in slightly to hear you.

He's deciding whether the bar is worth it.

fields.title: Darius
fields.body:
He's still standing there. Arms still folded. He looks at his guy on the ground, then back at you, with the expression of a man who has just revised an estimate downward and is being professional about it.

He says his name is Darius. Says he's not here on Kings business — which is a specific thing to volunteer. Says the package was a personal opportunity and he misjudged the job. He's not apologising. He's accounting.

He looks at the package under your arm. Then at you.

Tell Dutch he says hello. Then he walks. No hurry. Doesn't check if you're watching.

fields.title: Squeeze
fields.body:
You tell him you're there for the woman on the third floor. That whatever she owes, she doesn't owe it anymore. You don't raise your voice. You don't need to — the point isn't volume, it's clarity. You look at him the way you look at something you've already decided about and let him work out the rest himself.

Terrell is listening. Behind the performance of not being impressed, something is doing the math.

fields.title: Roll Success
fields.body: Cunning check passed. You come out on Decker two blocks south of the patrol. They're still sitting at Fifth, not looking your way. Unit twelve is another three minutes on foot.

fields.title: Strip Hand Off
fields.body:
He circles the package like it might explain itself.

Ten seconds in the back. Voices.

He hands it back.

“You didn’t get this from us.”

fields.title: Leverage
fields.body:
You ask him about the envelopes. He goes still.

You tell him you're not here to make it worse. Dutch's operation running through the bar is quiet income on top of what the bar makes — not nothing, when the cellophane windows are stacking up. You're not telling him anything he doesn't already know. You're just saying it out loud so you're both looking at the same picture.

He doesn't like it. Liking it isn't the ask.

fields.title: Squeeze Success
fields.body: Nerve check passed. He holds it for a second. Then something in his posture settles, the way a bluff does when the other person calls it and there's nothing left to protect. He doesn't say anything. He doesn't have to.

fields.title: Squeeze Failure
fields.body: Nerve check failed. He laughs — short, sharp, meant to be seen. "I'm gonna crush you, you little twerp," he says. He means it.

fields.title: Sal Talks
fields.body:
He talks. Once he starts he doesn't stop for a while.

He inherited Corky's four years ago from Dennis, who was his closest friend for twenty years and who left town in a hurry for reasons Sal has never pressed on. The bar wasn't making money then. It's barely making money now. He's kept it going because it was Dennis's and because the regulars have nowhere else to go and because some part of him still thinks it might turn around.

The dealers bother him less than the principle. It's his bar. If he can't decide who comes in then it's just a bar he works at.

He stops. Looks at the cloth in his hands like he forgot he was holding it. Says he doesn't know why he's telling you this.

fields.title: The Delivery
fields.body:
A woman answers. Forties, precise, looks at the package and not at you. Takes it. Closes the door.

That's it. That's the job.

You stand in the stairwell for a moment. Your knuckles are split. There's a noise coming from behind the door that might be a scale. You don't know what was in the package. You still don't ask.

You take out your phone to call Morris. You can tell him exactly what happened — Darius, the Kings, the name dropped at the end. Dutch would want to know. Or you can keep it simple. Package delivered. Job done.

fields.title: The Fight
fields.body:
Terrell squares up before you've finished closing the distance. He's done this before — you can see it in how he sets his feet, how his hands come up. He's got reach and he knows it. What he doesn't have is patience.

Somewhere above you a window slides shut. The city, taking no position.

fields.title: Roll Fail
fields.body:
Cunning check failed. You misread the back streets and come out directly across from the patrol. One of the officers clocks you before you can adjust — the package tucked under your arm, you moving a little too deliberately for a man with nowhere to be.

He doesn't stop you. He just watches. Writes something down.

fields.title: Something Off
fields.body:
The timestamps don’t line up.

That means either the job is wrong or the job is working exactly as intended.

You’re already too far in to find out which.

fields.title: Counter Offer
fields.body:
The offer is simple. Sal keeps Corky's clean on weeknights — his regulars, his rules. Weekend afternoons, Dutch's people can work the room. It's not what Dutch asked for but it's not nothing, and more importantly it's an arrangement Sal can live with. Operations that run on resentment leak. Dutch knows that, even if you've never met Dutch and are making an educated guess about what Dutch knows.

Sal writes it down on a bar napkin. You don't tell him that doesn't make it a contract. He needs the napkin to be official, so the napkin is official.

You take a photo of it on your phone. Tell him you'll pass it up the chain. Tell him Dutch is a reasonable man.

fields.title: Taken
fields.body:
You come back to yourself on the pavement. The package is gone. Darius is gone. His guys are gone. The street looks exactly like it did before — cars at the kerb, windows closed, nobody saw anything.

You call Morris. He listens to the whole thing without interrupting. When you finish he says he'll call you back. He doesn't call back.

fields.title: Sal Folds Intimidated
fields.body:
He says fine. One word. Then he moves to the other end of the bar and starts restocking glasses and doesn't look at you again.

You sit there a moment longer than you need to. The two old men haven't moved. The jukebox is between songs.

fields.title: Done Straight
fields.body:
Morris calls back in an hour. Dutch wants the name again — Darius — and the description. You give both. Morris asks if he said anything else. You tell him about the not on Kings business. Another pause, longer than the first.

The three hundred comes through plus a fifty on top. Information tax, Morris calls it, in a tone that suggests he finds this categorisation accurate and sufficient.

You don't know what Dutch does with the name. You told the truth and got paid for it.

fields.title: Backed Off
fields.body:
Terrell thinks about it for a moment — genuinely thinks, which is more than you expected — and then he pockets his phone and goes. No exit line. No last look. The ones who are actually scared never need the exit line.

You text Vickie two words. It's done.

She replies in three minutes with a thumbs up. A single thumbs up, after three years. You stare at it longer than makes sense and then put your phone away.

fields.title: Main Road
fields.body:
You walk past them. They see you. One of them gets on the radio. You don't run — running ends this differently — so you keep moving, even, unhurried, eyes forward, and count the seconds until you're past the intersection.

The package gets there. So does whatever they radioed in.

fields.title: New Build
fields.body:
Clean storefronts. Old problems underneath.

A man steps into your path.

“That’s not yours,” he says.

No anger. Just certainty.

fields.title: Sal Folds Leveraged
fields.body:
He looks at the shelf for a long time. Then back at you. Then he gives a small nod — not agreement exactly, more like a man accepting a diagnosis he already suspected.

He says the dealers can come back. He says it like a business decision, which is what you gave him — a frame that wasn't pure capitulation. You leave him to it.

fields.title: Sal Holds Firm
fields.body:
He's not moving. The decision is already made and whatever you say next is noise. Some people get to a point where the fear stops working and all that's left is the thing they're protecting.

You don't push it further. You tell him Dutch will be in touch. He says he knows. You leave.

fields.title: Done Covered
fields.body:
Three hundred by end of day, same as promised. Morris doesn't ask for details. You didn't offer any.

Somewhere in the Westside, Darius is telling someone about a courier who wouldn't go down. Maybe he's telling the Kings council. Maybe he's keeping it private, same as you. You won't know until it matters.

The split knuckles take three days to close.

fields.title: Roughed Up Win
fields.body:
Terrell's on the ground. His friends, who had opinions about this earlier, have revised them and left. You go through his pockets while he's still deciding whether to do something about that — a hundred and fifty in crumpled bills and a pair of brass knuckles with somebody else's initials scratched into the base, which tells a small story you don't have time for right now.

Your ribs are going to have thoughts about this tomorrow. You pocket both and go.

You text Vickie. It's done. The message sits on read.

fields.title: Done Dealers In
fields.body:
Morris calls that evening. Two hundred for the job. The dealers go back into Corky's starting Thursday. He says Dutch is satisfied, which from Morris means he says satisfied and hangs up.

The money goes in the drawer. There's another slip on the corkboard already — Morris works fast.

You don't go back to the bar. You don't have a reason to. Somewhere on the other side of the Flats, Sal is restocking glasses. The two old men at the end of the bar are still there, or they've gone home, and either way they'll be back tomorrow and the dealers will be back Thursday and nobody will say anything about it because that's the thing about Corky's. People learned a long time ago what not to notice.

fields.title: Done Lost
fields.body:
Morris doesn't call back that day. Or the next.

On the third day, a single text. Dutch wants the debt covered in work, not cash. There'll be a job. It won't be a good one.

The package is in whatever hands Darius was moving it to, doing whatever it was going to do. The corkboard is empty. You wait for Morris to put something on it.

fields.title: Done Clean
fields.body:
Back in the apartment. Radiator having its opinions. The boot-shaped water stain unchanged, indifferent, exactly where you left it.

You didn't have to break anything tonight. That used to be the baseline and somewhere along the way it became an achievement, which is either personal growth or a bad sign and you're not ready to decide which.

Vickie sends another message an hour later. Just: thank you. No punctuation, which means she agonised over it. You know her. She probably typed it six different ways.

The corkboard on the back of the door has one job pinned to it. The city is paying attention. That's something.

fields.title: Clean Delivery
fields.body:
The lockup smells like motor oil and old cardboard. Unit twelve has a combination padlock with the code scratched into the doorframe in pencil, which is either careless or confident. You set the package inside, lock up, and text the number Morris gave you. Delivered.

He replies in forty seconds. Acceptable. Then: Dutch knows your name. That's worth something. Then nothing.

There's a switchblade on the shelf above where you set the package. No note. You take it — it would be strange not to.

Two hundred cash arrives in the usual way, which you have now figured out is the usual way.

fields.title: Push Through
fields.body:
He doesn’t stop you.

That’s the whole decision.

You walk past.

fields.title: Done Sal Holds
fields.body:
Morris calls back two hours later. Dutch looked at the counter-offer. The answer is no — the dealers go back in on Dutch's terms or the conversation becomes a different one. There's no payout on this one.

Morris says Dutch appreciated the initiative. He says it the way he says everything: level, informational, not quite warm. He says there'll be more work. He says it carefully.

You still have the photo of the napkin on your phone. Sal built a counter-offer on a bar napkin and it lasted two hours.

fields.title: Done Rough
fields.body:
Back in the apartment. Ribs announcing themselves every time you breathe wrong. The hundred and fifty from Terrell's pocket on the counter next to the brass knuckles with the stranger's initials, which you should probably get rid of and probably won't.

Vickie never texted back. That's fine. You didn't do it for the reply.

The corkboard on the back of the door has one job pinned to it. Not a bad Tuesday, all things considered. The bar for that is low, but it's a bar.

fields.title: Done Sal Caved
fields.body:
Dutch handled it himself. Morris doesn't elaborate on what that means and you don't ask. What you know is that two days later the dealers are back in Corky's and Sal is still behind the bar and nothing visible has changed.

Morris sends a hundred. Half rate. You showed up and made the ask. That's apparently worth something — fifty less than something, but something.

Sal's still got the bar. That's probably what he'd say if you asked him.

fields.title: Beaten
fields.body:
You come to on the pavement outside Vickie's building, which is not where you intended to end up tonight. Ribs aching, wallet lighter — someone took the liberty while you were unavailable to object. The stoop light is still flickering. It was flickering before. It'll be flickering tomorrow.

You don't go up. You can't do that conversation right now, can't stand in Vickie's doorway looking like this and tell her it's handled. You just walk. The Flats swallows you up the way it always has — wide streets, low buildings, sky overhead doing nothing in particular — and after a while you stop thinking about it.

Your phone has no new messages.

fields.title: Hot Delivery
fields.body:
Package delivered. Morris's reply takes four minutes this time. Delivered. Blues logged a person of interest on Decker carrying a package. You. Pause. Dutch is aware. Another pause. A hundred and fifty.

Fifty less than agreed. You don't argue. There's nobody to argue with — Morris has already hung up.

The lockup has a switchblade on the shelf. You look at it. You don't take it. That feels right.

fields.title: The Drop
fields.body:
Clean.

That’s the only word for it.

A man takes the package. Doesn’t check it.

Ten seconds later, it’s gone.

“You’re done.”

fields.title: Clocked
fields.body:
Package delivered. Morris's reply comes in three minutes. Delivered. Blues patrol noted someone on the back route near Marsh. Description matches. A pause. A hundred and seventy-five.

Twenty-five short. You did everything right except the one thing. Morris doesn't explain the math — he doesn't need to. Dutch's people are careful because Dutch is careful, and the back route is supposed to be clean, and now there's a description floating around that matches yours.

fields.title: Parked Car
fields.body:
The car has been there long enough to belong.

You pass it once.

Nothing.

The second time, you feel it.

Not attention. Recognition.

You keep walking.

fields.title: Crossing Lines
fields.body:
Back home.

Cash. Zonk.

The route stays in your head.

Morris said acceptable.

That’s what you get.
